- **FIR application:** Diagnostic ECG filtering — preserves waveform morphology (linear phase is essential for accurate ST-segment analysis).
- **IIR application:** Real-time wearable heart-rate monitor — requires low computational load and can tolerate mild phase distortion since only R-peak detection is needed.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A — Choice 1** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**With neat block diagrams, explain the complete biosignal processing pipeline used in a myoelectric prosthetic arm. Discuss in detail the role of each block in ensuring accurate control of the prosthesis.**

#### **Part (a) — Signal Acquisition and Analog Conditioning (7 marks)**

**Answer:**

A myoelectric prosthetic arm processes surface EMG (sEMG) signals from the residual limb to decode user intent. The pipeline consists of **six cascaded blocks**:

**1. Electrode Interface (1 mark)**
- **Ag/AgCl surface electrodes** placed in differential configuration over the agonist-antagonist muscle pair.
- **Differential recording** rejects common-mode noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum).

**2. Instrumentation Amplifier (2 marks)**
- Three-op-amp INA topology (e.g., AD620).
- **High CMRR (≥ 80 dB)** is critical because EMG amplitudes (~50 µV – 5 mV) are easily swamped by environmental interference.
- Gain setting: $G = 1 + \dfrac{49.4 k\Omega}{R_G}$, typically set to **G = 1000**.

**3. Analog Band-Pass Filter (2 marks)**
- **HPF (fc = 20 Hz):** Removes motion artifact and DC offset due to electrode-skin interface.
- **LPF (fc = 500 Hz):** Removes high-frequency noise and RF interference.
- **Notch Filter (50 Hz):** Specifically attenuates Indian power-line interference.

**4. ADC and DSP (2 marks)**
- Sampling at **≥ 1000 Hz** (Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 \times 500$).
- 12 to 16-bit resolution (quantization step ≤ 2.44 mV acceptable for EMG).

#### **Part (b) — Feature Extraction, Classification, and Actuation (7 marks)**

**Answer:**

**1. Rectification and Smoothing (1 mark)**
- **Full-wave rectification** followed by **envelope detection** (low-pass filtering with fc ≈ 8 Hz) to obtain a slowly-varying amplitude profile proportional to muscle contraction level.

**2. Time-Domain Feature Extraction (2 marks)**
- **Mean Absolute Value (MAV):** $\;MAV = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert x_i \vert$
- **Root Mean Square (RMS):** $\;RMS = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}$
- **Waveform Length:** $\;WL = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \vert x_{i+1} - x_i \vert$
- **Zero Crossings (ZC)** and **Slope Sign Changes (SSC)** — frequency-content indicators.

**3. Classification (2 marks)**
- **Threshold-based:** Simple — if MAV > Threshold → "Open Grip"; else → "Close Grip".
- **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA):** Multi-class — separates "Wrist Flexion," "Wrist Extension," "Hand Open," "Hand Close" using linear decision boundaries.
- **Advanced:** SVM or Neural Networks for multi-finger dexterity.

**4. Actuation and Feedback (2 marks)**
- The classified command is sent to a **DC motor / servo** in the prosthetic hand.
- **Force/position feedback sensors** provide closed-loop control.
- **Proportional-Integral (PI) controller** adjusts motor torque proportional to EMG amplitude — enabling **proportional speed/grip control**, the hallmark of modern myoelectric devices.

---

### ⭐ **Question B — Choice 2** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Explain the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem in detail. An EEG signal is bandlimited to 70 Hz. Determine the minimum sampling frequency. If the sampling rate is reduced to 100 Hz, what phenomenon occurs? Show mathematically how the aliased frequency can be computed.**

#### **Part (a) — Nyquist-Shannon Theorem (7 marks)**

**Answer:**

**Statement (2 marks):**
A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ can be **uniquely and perfectly reconstructed** from its discrete samples $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ if and only if the sampling frequency $f_s = 1/T_s$ satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

The minimum sampling rate, $f_{Nyquist} = 2 f_{max}$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**.

**Mathematical Basis (3 marks):**
Sampling in the time domain corresponds to **periodic replication** of the spectrum in the frequency domain at intervals of $f_s$:

$$X_s(f) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

For no overlap of spectral replicas (no aliasing), the replicas must be non-overlapping, requiring $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

**Anti-Aliasing Filter (2 marks):**
A practical LPF (anti-aliasing filter) is placed **before the ADC** to bandlimit the analog signal to $f_{max}$, ensuring the Nyquist condition is satisfied for the dominant spectral content.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation and Aliasing (7 marks)**

**Answer:**

**1. Minimum Sampling Frequency (2 marks)**

Given $f_{max} = 70$ Hz for EEG:

$$f_{s,min} = 2 \times 70 = \boxed{140 \text{ Hz}}$$

Practical systems use $f_s = 256$ Hz or $512$ Hz to account for filter roll-off.

**2. Aliasing Phenomenon (2 marks)**
If $f_s = 100$ Hz (which is $< 2 f_{max} = 140$ Hz), the spectral replicas **overlap**, causing **aliasing** — high-frequency EEG content folds back into the low-frequency range, corrupting the signal.

**3. Computing the Aliased Frequency (3 marks)**

Consider an EEG component at $f_{true} = 60$ Hz. Its image in the baseband $[0, f_s/2]$ is given by:

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{true} - k \cdot f_s \vert$$

For the smallest non-negative value, choose $k$ such that the result is $\leq f_s/2 = 50$ Hz.

With $k = 1$:

$$f_{alias} = \vert 60 - 100 \vert = 40 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{alias} = 40 \text{ Hz}}$$

**4. Consequence (extra, for full marks)**
A genuine 60 Hz alpha-band EEG component **falsely appears** as a 40 Hz signal — clinically meaningless. The BCI controller would misinterpret brain state, leading to **erroneous wheelchair or prosthetic commands** — a safety hazard.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in This Topic**
>
> 1. **Confusing linear and dB SNR formulas.** Use $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratio, $20 \log_{10}$ for amplitude ratio. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for the wrong one.
> 2. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter** in pipeline diagrams. The ADC block is NOT the first filter — always precede it with an analog LPF.
> 3. **Mixing up FIR and IIR properties.** "FIR is always stable" and "IIR has linear phase" are **false statements** that fetch negative marks.
> 4. **Skipping units** in the final answer. Always write Hz, dB, mV — unitless numerical answers signal carelessness.
> 5. **Computing aliasing without showing the modulo operation.** The formula $f_{alias} = \vert f_{true} - k f_s \vert$ must be explicitly written and the choice of $k$ justified.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorize These Before Entering the Exam Hall**

- [x] **SNR formula** — power ratio → 10 log; amplitude ratio → 20 log.
- [x] **Nyquist** — sample at **≥ 2×** the highest signal frequency to avoid **aliasing**.
- [x] **Aliasing** — high frequencies fold back; cure = increase $f_s$ or add **anti-aliasing LPF**.
- [x] **Quantization step** = $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$ — **higher $n$ = better resolution**.
- [x] **Filter cutoffs** for ECG: 0.05 Hz HPF, 150 Hz LPF, 50 Hz notch.
- [x] **Filter cutoffs** for EEG: 0.5 Hz HPF, 70 Hz LPF, 50 Hz notch.
- [x] **Filter cutoffs** for EMG: 20 Hz HPF, 500 Hz LPF, 50 Hz notch.
- [x] **FIR = linear phase, always stable**; **IIR = recursive, lower order, faster**.
- [x] **Signal averaging** improves SNR by a factor of $\sqrt{M}$; in dB, gain is $10 \log_{10} M$.
- [x] **FFT** is an efficient $O(N \log N)$ implementation of the DFT — the standard tool for frequency-domain biosignal analysis.
- [x] **CMRR > 80 dB** is mandatory in any instrumentation amplifier for biosignal acquisition.
- [x] **Common features extracted** in myoelectric control: MAV, RMS, WL, ZC, SSC.
- [x] **Pipeline order** (memorize this sequence): Electrode → INA → HPF → LPF → Notch → ADC → DSP → Feature Extract → Classify → Actuate.
- [x] **Real-world assistive devices** tested in KTU papers: Cochlear implant (audio processing), myoelectric arm (EMG), BCI wheelchair (EEG), EOG-based communication (eye signals).

---

> **END OF NOTES — OEBMT722 MODULE 2: Data Processing and Signal Interpretation**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Standards | NEP 2020 Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobility Aids: Wheelchairs, Scooters, Walkers, Prosthetics, and Orthotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT722 - ASSISTIVE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mobility Aids: Wheelchairs, Scooters, Walkers, Prosthetics, and Orthotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Mobility Aids — Formal KTU 2024 Definition

**Mobility Aids** are externally applied assistive devices designed to enhance, restore, or substitute the locomotive function of individuals with impairments of the musculoskeletal, neuromuscular, or skeletal systems. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *OEBMT722 – Assistive Devices*, mobility aids are classified into two primary super-categories:

1. **Mobility Vehicles (Restorative Locomotion Devices):** Wheelchairs, Powered Scooters, and Walkers.
2. **Wearable Body-Powered or Externally Powered Substitutes:** Prosthetics (replacing missing limbs) and Orthotics (supporting/aligning existing limbs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize:**
> A *prosthetic device* replaces an absent body part, whereas an *orthotic device* provides support, alignment, or correction to an existing body segment. This distinction is a **favourite 2-mark/3-mark question** in KTU board exams.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a mobility aid as a **"mechanical extension of the human skeleton"**, much like a crane extends a human arm. Just as a crane supplements weak muscles to lift heavy loads, a wheelchair or prosthesis supplements impaired bones, joints, or muscles to enable movement.

- **Wheelchair** → Imagine a "rolling chair that becomes a second pair of legs" when the legs cannot bear weight. It transfers the support base from the feet to a wheeled frame.
- **Scooter** → Think of a "miniature electric vehicle with a captain's chair" — designed for users who can sit upright and steer with their hands but cannot walk long distances.
- **Walker** → Visualize a "stable metal cage" the user pushes ahead of them — essentially, a balance frame that widens the base of support.
- **Prosthesis** → A "mechanical ghost limb" — an artificial surrogate for a missing anatomical segment.
- **Orthosis** → A "mechanical brace" — an external scaffold that supports a real, weakened limb.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Quantitative Reference Standards

The following **standard quantitative metrics** are mandated in the KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Average adult walking speed | $v_{walk}$ | **1.3 – 1.5** | m/s |
| Standard wheelchair doorway clearance (ADA) | $W_{door}$ | **≥ 815** | mm |
| Maximum ramp slope (ADA compliance) | $\theta_{ramp}$ | **1:12 (≈ 4.76°)** | ratio |
| Static stability angle (wheelchair) | $\phi_{stab}$ | **≥ 10°** | degrees |
| ISO 7176 Wheelchair classification | – | **Class A, B, C** | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomechanical Constant:** The standard **human lower-limb length proportion** is **~ 0.53 × body height**. This constant is critical when designing prosthetics for correct gait symmetry.

## 1.4 WHO & ICF Classification Hook

The **International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (ICF)** by the WHO classifies mobility impairment under the domain *Activities & Participation — Chapter 4 (Mobility, d450–d469)*. Mobility aids directly address the ICF qualifier domains:

- **Body Function (b):** Restoration of gait kinematics.
- **Activity (d):** Re-enabling `d450 Walking`, `d455 Moving around`, `d465 Moving around using equipment`.
- **Participation (p):** Restoring community ambulation.

This is the **NEP 2020 alignment** KTU examiners expect for the *Humanitarian Engineering* context of the OEBMT722 course.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Wheelchairs — Operational Taxonomy

A wheelchair is a manually or motor-propelled chair mounted on wheels, used by individuals unable to walk due to illness, injury, or disability. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires the following classification to be memorized.

### 2.1.1 Manual Wheelchair Sub-Types

| Type | Drive Mechanism | User Profile | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard Manual (Self-Propelled)** | Rear-rim propulsion by user | Bilateral UE strength | Adult paraplegics |
| **Transit/Attendant-Propelled** | Pushed by caregiver | No UE function | Hospital wards, airports |
| **Folding (Cross-Fold)** | X-frame hinged mechanism | Portability users | Travellers |
| **Rigid-Frame (Performance)** | Fixed-frame, low mass (<8 kg) | Active users | Sports, daily |
| **Tilt-in-Space** | Fixed seat angle, entire chair tilts | Pressure-sore risk | Cerebral palsy, elderly |
| **Reclining** | Backrest angle variable | Hip-flexion contracture | Post-surgery |

### 2.1.2 Powered Wheelchair Sub-Types

- **Class A (Indoor):** Compact, tight turning radius (<1.0 m), speed ≤ 6 km/h.
- **Class B (Mixed Indoor/Outdoor):** Moderate power, speed ≤ 10 km/h.
- **Class C (Outdoor):** Robust, ≥ 4 wheels, high torque, speed ≤ 15 km/h.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Hook:** *"Differentiate between Class A, B, and C powered wheelchairs"* is a classic **2-mark or 3-mark question**. Memorize the use-environment and speed limits.

## 2.2 Mobility Scooters

A **mobility scooter** (Class 2 or Class 3 in UK classification) is a 3- or 4-wheeled battery-powered vehicle with a steering tiller, designed for ambulatory users with reduced endurance.

**Key engineering specifications:**

$$v_{scooter} = 6 \text{ to } 15 \text{ km/h}$$

$$P_{motor} = 200 \text{ to } 750 \text{ W}$$

$$R_{range} = 25 \text{ to } 60 \text{ km per charge}$$

$$Q_{battery} = 35 \text{ to } 75 \text{ Ah (Lead-Acid / Li-ion)}$$

> **Scooter vs Powered Wheelchair — KTU Distinction:**
> - Scooter → user must have **trunk control**, **sitting balance**, and **intact cognition** (steered via tiller).
> - Powered wheelchair → joystick-controlled, can be used by users with **poor trunk control** or **cognitive impairment**.

## 2.3 Walkers (Ambulation Aids)

Walkers are external frames that enlarge the user's **base of support (BoS)**, providing bilateral upper-extremity support during gait.

### 2.3.1 Walkers — Engineering Sub-Types

| Sub-Type | Wheels | Brakes | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Standard / Pick-Up Walker** | 0 | None | Lightest, must be lifted |
| **Two-Wheeled Walker** | 2 (front) | None | Partial lift, partial roll |
| **Rollator (Four-Wheeled)** | 4 | Hand brakes | Seat, basket, 3-in-1 |
| **Hemi-Walker** | 4 | None | One-sided, hemiplegia |
| **Knee Walker (Knee Scooter)** | 4 | Hand brake | Platform for shin — foot/ankle injuries |
| **Platform Walker** | 4 | Optional | Forearm platform — RA patients |

### 2.3.2 Walkers — Stability Principle

The **biomechanical rule** for walker stability is:

$$A_{BoS,walker} > A_{BoS,user}$$

where the **base of support** of the walker is the polygon formed by the contact points of the wheels/legs. A wider BoS increases the **static stability margin** $\phi_{stab}$, defined as the minimum angle through which the resultant ground-reaction force can be tilted before the user's combined center of gravity (CoG) exits the support polygon.

## 2.4 Prosthetics (Limb Substitutes)

A **prosthesis** is an artificial device that replaces a missing body part, typically a limb lost through amputation, trauma, or congenital deficiency.

### 2.4.1 Lower-Limb Prosthetic Levels (KTU High-Yield)

| Amputation Level | Prosthetic Component Set |
|---|---|
| **Trans-tibial (Below Knee — BK)** | Socket + Pylon + SACH/Foot |
| **Knee Disarticulation** | Socket + Hinged knee + Pylon + Foot |
| **Trans-femoral (Above Knee — AK)** | Socket + Knee unit + Pylon + Foot |
| **Hip Disarticulation** | Hip joint + Knee + Pylon + Foot |
| **Trans-pelvic (Hemicorporectomy)** | Custom socket + Hip + Knee + Foot |

### 2.4.2 Upper-Limb Prosthetic Levels

- **Trans-radial (Below Elbow)** — Most common.
- **Trans-humeral (Above Elbow)** — Includes elbow unit.
- **Shoulder Disarticulation** — Includes shoulder unit.

### 2.4.3 Prosthetic Control Mechanisms (KTU Differentiator)

| Class | Control Input | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Body-Powered** | Cable + harness (shoulder/scapular motion) | Durable, low cost, high proprioceptive feedback |
| **Externally Powered (Myoelectric)** | Surface EMG electrodes | Cosmetic, higher grip force |
| **Hybrid** | Body + myoelectric combined | Bilateral amputees |
| **Activity-Specific** | Task-specific (swim, golf, etc.) | Sports prostheses |

## 2.5 Orthotics (Limb Supports)

An **orthosis** is an externally applied device that influences the structural and functional characteristics of the neuromuscular and skeletal systems.

### 2.5.1 Standard KTU Acronym Table (Mandatory for Exam)

| Acronym | Full Form | Region Covered |
|---|---|---|
| **FO** | Foot Orthosis | Foot only |
| **AFO** | Ankle-Foot Orthosis | Ankle + Foot |
| **KAFO** | Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthosis | Knee + Ankle + Foot |
| **HKAFO** | Hip-Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthosis | Hip + Knee + Ankle + Foot |
| **KO** | Knee Orthosis | Knee only |
| **HO** | Hip Orthosis | Hip only |
| **WHO** | Wrist-Hand Orthosis | Wrist + Hand |
| **EO** | Elbow Orthosis | Elbow |
| **SEWHO** | Shoulder-Elbow-Wrist-Hand Orthosis | Upper limb full |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the *expansion* of acronyms FO, AFO, KAFO, HKAFO. Always write the full form **before** the abbreviation, in line with KTU valuation key style.

### 2.5.2 Material Categories

- **Metal + Leather** (Conventional) — durable, heavy, used for polio and high-load cases.
- **Thermoplastic** (Polypropylene, polyethylene) — custom-molded, lightweight, modern standard.
- **Carbon-Fiber Composite** — high strength-to-weight ratio, dynamic-response feet.
- **3D-Printed (PLA / Nylon)** — emerging rapid-prototyping standard.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (All-in-One Reference)

The following table consolidates **every quantitative equation** required for KTU 2024 OEBMT722 Module 3 numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static Stability Margin (Walker) | $\tan \phi_{stab} = \dfrac{d_{CoG}}{h_{CoG}}$ | $d_{CoG}$ = horizontal CoG offset (m); $h_{CoG}$ = CoG height (m) | Larger margin ⇒ safer |
| 2 | Energy Cost of Walking (per kg per m) | $C_{walk} = \dfrac{W_{met}}{m \cdot d}$ | $W_{met}$ = metabolic energy (J); $m$ = body mass (kg); $d$ = distance (m) | Healthy ≈ **4.0 J/kg/m**; amputee ≈ **8–12 J/kg/m** |
| 3 | Wheelchair Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot N$ | $\mu_{rr}$ = rolling resistance coeff. (≈ 0.005–0.015); $N$ = normal load (N) | Pneumatic tyres lower $\mu_{rr}$ |
| 4 | Powered Wheelchair Power | $P = F_{total} \cdot v$ | $F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{slope} + F_{aero}$ (N); $v$ = velocity (m/s) | Sum all resistance forces |
| 5 | Slope Force Component | $F_{slope} = m g \sin \theta$ | $m$ = mass (kg); $g$ = 9.81 m/s²; $\theta$ = slope angle (rad) | ADA max = 4.76° (1:12) |
| 6 | Cadence | $f_{step} = \dfrac{N_{steps}}{t}$ | $N_{steps}$ = number of steps; $t$ = time (s) | Normal ≈ **1.7–2.0 Hz** |
| 7 | Stride Length | $L_{stride} = v / f_{step}$ | $v$ = velocity (m/s); $f_{step}$ = cadence (Hz) | Healthy adult ≈ 1.4 m |
| 8 | Gait Symmetry Index (SI) | $SI = \dfrac{\vert x_{L} - x_{R} \vert}{0.5 (x_{L} + x_{R})} \times 100\%$ | $x_{L}, x_{R}$ = left/right stride parameters | 0% = perfect symmetry |
| 9 | Prosthetic Socket Pressure (Pylon Loading) | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_{socket}}$ | $F$ = axial load (N); $A_{socket}$ = cross-section (m²) | Must remain < 200 kPa at residuum |
| 10 | Battery Range | $R = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{P_{draw}}$ | $V$ = voltage (V); $Q$ = capacity (Ah); $P_{draw}$ = power draw (W) | Constant-load approximation |
| 11 | Three-Point Pressure System (Orthosis) | $\sum F_{load} = F_{c1} + F_{c2} + F_{c3}$ | Three counter-forces create corrective moment | Principle behind all AFO/KAFO designs |
| 12 | Donning & Doffing Torque | $\tau = r \times F$ | $r$ = moment arm (m); $F$ = force (N) | Used in prosthetic alignment |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark-Allocation Tip:** Numericals in Module 3 are usually **5–7 mark** problems, and they will always provide values so you can plug into the equations above. Memorize the equations; the values are gifts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Cost of Walking with a Prosthesis vs. Normal Gait

The KTU 2024 Scheme frequently asks students to **derive or compare the metabolic cost of ambulation** between a normal-limb subject and a unilateral trans-tibial amputee using a prosthesis. Below is the full step-by-step derivation expected in the 14-mark valuation.

### 3.1.1 Given (Standard KTU Problem Statement)

- A unilateral trans-tibial amputee of mass $m = 70$ kg walks a horizontal distance $d = 100$ m at $v = 1.0$ m/s.
- Metabolic energy expenditure rate $W_{met} = 350$ W (with prosthesis).
- Healthy control of the same mass: $W_{met,healthy} = 200$ W.
- Compute:
  (a) The **metabolic cost per kilogram per metre** for the amputee.
  (b) The **percentage increase** in metabolic cost compared to a healthy individual.
  (c) The **cadence** in Hz if stride length is $L_s = 1.0$ m.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Time taken to walk 100 m**

$$ t = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{100 \text{ m}}{1.0 \text{ m/s}} = 100 \text{ s} $$

> *Reasoning:* Distance divided by speed gives the duration of the activity. This will be used to convert power into total energy.

**Step 2 — Total metabolic energy expended by amputee**

$$ W_{met,total} = W_{met} \times t = 350 \text{ W} \times 100 \text{ s} $$

$$ W_{met,total} = 35{,}000 \text{ J} = 35 \text{ kJ} $$

> *Reasoning:* Power is energy per unit time, so energy equals power multiplied by duration.

**Step 3 — Cost of walking per kg per metre (amputee)**

$$ C_{walk,amputee} = \frac{W_{met,total}}{m \cdot d} = \frac{35{,}000 \text{ J}}{70 \text{ kg} \times 100 \text{ m}} $$

$$ C_{walk,amputee} = \frac{35{,}000}{7{,}000} = 5.0 \text{ J/(kg·m)} $$

> *Reasoning:* This is the standardised KTU metric for comparing ambulation efficiency across subjects of different body mass. A healthy adult typically scores 3.5–4.0 J/(kg·m); the amputee shows a 25–40% elevation.

**Step 4 — Cost of walking for healthy control (verification)**

$$ t_{healthy} = \frac{100}{1.0} = 100 \text{ s} $$

$$ W_{healthy,total} = 200 \text{ W} \times 100 \text{ s} = 20{,}000 \text{ J} = 20 \text{ kJ} $$

$$ C_{walk,healthy} = \frac{20{,}000}{70 \times 100} = \frac{20{,}000}{7{,}000} \approx 2.86 \text{ J/(kg·m)} $$

**Step 5 — Percentage increase in metabolic cost**

$$ \Delta C\% = \frac{C_{amputee} - C_{healthy}}{C_{healthy}} \times 100\% $$

$$ \Delta C\% = \frac{5.0 - 2.86}{2.86} \times 100\% $$

$$ \Delta C\% = \frac{2.14}{2.86} \times 100\% \approx 74.8\% $$

> *Reasoning:* A ~75% increase is realistic for an unaccustomed trans-tibial amputee. With training, this reduces to 25–40%.

**Step 6 — Cadence calculation**

$$ f_{step} = \frac{v}{L_s} = \frac{1.0 \text{ m/s}}{1.0 \text{ m}} = 1.0 \text{ Hz} $$

> *Reasoning:* Cadence is the step frequency. Each step covers one stride length in the simple gait model; therefore $v = L_s \cdot f_{step}$.

### 3.1.3 KTU Valuation Standard — Final Answer Layout

| Part | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (a) | Energy cost of amputee | $C_{amputee}$ | **5.0** | J/(kg·m) |
| (b) | % increase over healthy | $\Delta C$ | **74.8** | % |
| (c) | Cadence | $f_{step}$ | **1.0** | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Always include units in every KTU numerical.** Examiners award 0.5 marks specifically for correct unit annotation in Module 3 problems.

## 3.2 Derivation: Three-Point Pressure Principle in a KAFO (Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthosis)

The Three-Point Pressure System is the foundational biomechanical principle used to design any orthosis. A KTU 14-mark question on this topic requires deriving the **net corrective moment** about the knee.

### 3.2.1 Given

- A KAFO applies a corrective varus moment to a knee with a flexion contracture.
- Three forces are applied:
  - $F_1 = 50$ N at the thigh cuff (proximal lateral).
  - $F_2 = 50$ N at the calf band (distal medial).
  - $F_3$ at the knee joint line (medial side).
- Moment arms: $r_1 = 0.20$ m, $r_2 = 0.15$ m, $r_3 = 0.04$ m.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the three-point principle**

For static equilibrium of moments about the knee:

$$ \sum M_{knee} = 0 $$

$$ F_1 \cdot r_1 - F_2 \cdot r_2 - F_3 \cdot r_3 = 0 $$

> *Reasoning:* Two forces create the bending couple; the third is the counter-force at the joint line to maintain equilibrium. Convention: counter-clockwise positive.

**Step 2 — Solve for $F_3$**

$$ F_3 = \frac{F_1 r_1 - F_2 r_2}{r_3} $$

$$ F_3 = \frac{(50)(0.20) - (50)(0.15)}{0.04} $$

$$ F_3 = \frac{10 - 7.5}{0.04} = \frac{2.5}{0.04} $$

$$ F_3 = 62.5 \text{ N} $$

> *Reasoning:* The medial counter-force at the knee must exceed 60 N to produce a net varus corrective moment — within the skin-pressure tolerance (typically ≤ 100 kPa on a 25 cm² contact patch ≈ 250 N max).

**Step 3 — Verify against the skin-pressure limit**

$$ P_{skin} = \frac{F_3}{A_{contact}} = \frac{62.5}{0.0025} = 25{,}000 \text{ Pa} = 25 \text{ kPa} $$

> *Reasoning:* Capillary closure pressure ≈ 4.3 kPa (32 mmHg) for sustained pressure; 25 kPa is acceptable for short-duration donning. KTU examiners expect this safety check.

**Step 4 — State the design rule**

> **KTU Design Rule (Memorize):** In any three-point system, the **middle force is opposite in direction and has the smallest moment arm**; the outer forces are equal-and-opposite and have the **largest moment arms** to maximize mechanical advantage.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Assessment

This matrix is the **highest-value revision tool** for KTU 2024 module-level viva and 14-markers.

| Parameter | Manual Wheelchair | Powered Wheelchair | Scooter | Walker | Prosthesis | Orthosis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Substitute gait | Substitute gait | Substitute gait (endurance) | Assist gait | Replace missing limb | Support/align existing limb |
| **Energy Source** | User (UE) | Battery | Battery | User | User (or battery) | User (or stored) |
| **Cardio Demand** | High | Low | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Low |
| **UE Strength Required** | High | Low (joystick) | Low-Medium | Medium | Variable | Low |
| **Trunk Control Required** | Low | Low | High | Medium | High | Variable |
| **Cognitive Demand** | Low | Low-Medium | High (road safety) | Low | Medium | Low |
| **Cost (relative)** | Low | High | Medium-High | Low | High | Low-Medium |
| **KTU Use-Case Example** | Paraplegic daily | Quadriplegic, elderly | Outdoor errands | Post-op rehab | Trans-tibial amputee | Cerebral palsy (AFO) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree — Mobility Aids

The following Mermaid block is a **fully KTU-aligned, no-keyword-compile-risk** classification chart of all mobility aids covered in Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[MOBILITY AIDS MODULE 3]:::root --> B1[MOBILITY VEHICLES]
    A0 --> B2[WEARABLE SUBSTITUTES]

    B1 --> C1[Wheelchairs]
    B1 --> C2[Scooters]
    B1 --> C3[Walkers]

    C1 --> D1[Manual]
    C1 --> D2[Powered]
    D1 --> D1a[Self Propelled]
    D1 --> D1b[Attendant]
    D1 --> D1c[Folding]
    D1 --> D1d[Rigid Frame]
    D2 --> D2a[Class A Indoor]
    D2 --> D2b[Class B Mixed]
    D2 --> D2c[Class C Outdoor]

    C2 --> E1[Class 2 Pavement]
    C2 --> E2[Class 3 Road]

    C3 --> F1[Standard Pickup]
    C3 --> F2[Two Wheeled]
    C3 --> F3[Rollator]
    C3 --> F4[Knee Walker]
    C3 --> F5[Platform]

    B2 --> G1[Prosthetics]
    B2 --> G2[Orthotics]

    G1 --> H1[Upper Limb]
    G1 --> H2[Lower Limb]
    H1 --> H1a[Trans Radial]
    H1 --> H1b[Trans Humeral]
    H2 --> H2a[Trans Tibial BK]
    H2 --> H2b[Trans Femoral AK]
    H2 --> H2c[Hip Disarticulation]

    G2 --> I1[Lower Limb Orthoses]
    G2 --> I2[Upper Limb Orthoses]
    I1 --> I1a[FO]
    I1 --> I1b[AFO]
    I1 --> I1c[KAFO]
    I1 --> I1d[HKAFO]
    I2 --> I2a[WHO]
    I2 --> I2b[EO]
    I2 --> I2c[SEWHO]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e3a8a;
    classDef default fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e;
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Prosthetic Lower-Limb Assembly

The following diagram maps the **functional flow** of a trans-femoral prosthesis — the most-tested prosthesis in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Residual Limb]:::a --> S0[Socket Suspension Interface]:::a
    S0 --> S1[Pylon Shank]:::b
    S1 --> S2[Knee Unit Mechanism]:::c
    S2 --> S3[Pylon Tube]:::b
    S3 --> S4[Dynamic Response Foot]:::d
    S4 --> G0[Ground Reaction Force]:::e

    S2 --> CTRL1[Microprocessor or Hydraulic Control]:::f
    CTRL1 --> S2

    S0 --> B1[Biomechanical Feedback EMG]:::f
    B1 --> CTRL1

    classDef a fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#78350f
    classDef b fill:#bae6fd,stroke:#0369a1,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef c fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef d fill:#d9f99d,stroke:#4d7c0f,color:#365314
    classDef e fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#581c87
    classDef f fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#7c2d12
```

**Reading the architecture:**

1. The **residual limb** loads the **socket**, which transfers force to the **pylon**.
2. The **knee unit** (single-axis, polycentric, hydraulic, or microprocessor-controlled) modulates swing-phase and stance-phase flexion.
3. The **pylon tube** carries axial load to the **foot** (SACH, single-axis, multi-axial, or dynamic-response).
4. The **ground reaction force (GRF)** vector is transmitted back up the chain to the socket.
5. **EMG / micro-processor control** modulates the knee damping in real time (for advanced prostheses).

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Orthotic Three-Point Pressure System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J0[Affected Joint e.g. Knee]:::joint --> F1A[Force F1 Proximal Lateral]:::force
    J0 --> F2A[Force F3 Distal Medial Counterforce]:::force
    J0 --> F3A[Force F2 At Joint Line Medial]:::force
    F1A --> M0[Net Corrective Varus Moment]:::moment
    F2A --> M0
    F3A --> M0
    M0 --> SK[Skin Pressure Check less than 100 kPa]:::safety

    classDef joint fill:#fcd34d,stroke:#92400e,color:#78350f
    classDef force fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#991b1b,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef moment fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#14532d
    classDef safety fill:#c4b5fd,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#4c1d95
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram above represents the static force-couple used in every orthosis. KTU examiners frequently request a labelled sketch of the three forces; if you cannot draw, you must describe the diagram verbally in the answer script. The Mermaid flow above is the **acceptable substitute** under KTU 2024 OBE guidelines.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mobility Aids:**
> 1. **Confusing prosthetic vs. orthotic** — examiners deduct **1 full mark** if you interchange these in a definition.
> 2. **Skipping unit annotation** in numericals — **0.5 mark penalty** per missing unit.
> 3. **Forgetting the full form** of AFO/KAFO — write it out, the abbreviation alone is not enough.
> 4. **Ignoring the three-point pressure principle** in any KAFO/AFO design question — this is the *single highest-weighted concept* in Module 3.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between a prosthesis and an orthosis with one example each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Prosthesis | Orthosis |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | An artificial device that **replaces a missing body part** (limb or organ). | An external device that **supports, aligns, prevents, or corrects** deformities of an existing body segment. |
| **Function** | Restoration of absent function. | Augmentation or protection of existing function. |
| **Example** | A trans-tibial (below-knee) prosthesis replacing an amputated leg. | An Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO) controlling drop-foot in a stroke patient. |
| **Residuum** | The limb segment is **absent**. | The limb segment is **present but impaired**. |

> **Valuation key:** 1 mark each for the definitions, 1 mark for the examples. Without examples, expect 0.5 mark deduction.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"List and briefly explain any three types of manual wheelchairs."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per type):**

1. **Self-Propelled Manual Wheelchair** — Equipped with rear-mounted hand-rims; user propels by bilateral upper-extremity push strokes. Most common in KTU reference materials for paraplegic patients.
2. **Attendant-Propelled (Transit) Wheelchair** — Smaller rear wheels, no hand-rims; pushed by a caregiver. Used in hospitals, airports.
3. **Tilt-in-Space Wheelchair** — The seat-to-back angle is fixed, but the entire seat-back assembly tilts backwards (up to 60°). Indicated for users with poor trunk control or pressure-sore risk (e.g., cerebral palsy, advanced MS).
4. **Folding (Cross-Fold) Wheelchair** — X-frame hinged mechanism allows compact storage in car boots; standard for travellers.
5. **Rigid-Frame Performance Wheelchair** — Non-folding, low mass (< 8 kg), energy-efficient; used by active users and sportspersons.

> **Valuation key:** Naming + 1-line functional description = 1 mark each. Three correct entries earn full 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Wheelchairs, Walkers, Biomechanics Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **classification of wheelchairs** based on propulsion and control. Discuss the biomechanical advantages and limitations of **powered wheelchairs** over manual wheelchairs for a chronic paraplegic patient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 65-kg elderly patient uses a standard pickup walker and walks 50 m at 0.5 m/s. The walker applies a horizontal corrective force of 15 N at the hand-grip level. Compute the **energy expenditure per kg per metre** if the metabolic rate is 250 W. Comment on whether the **base-of-support** of the walker is sufficient for safe ambulation. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Classification of wheelchairs (sketch description in lieu of figure — KTU accepted):**

| Category | Type | Drive | User Profile |
|---|---|---|---|
| Manual | Self-Propelled | Rear hand-rim | Active user, UE strong |
| Manual | Attendant-Propelled | Caregiver push | Hospital, frail elderly |
| Manual | Folding | Self/Attendant | Traveller |
| Manual | Rigid-Frame | Self | Sports, performance |
| Manual | Tilt-in-Space | Attendant | Pressure care, CP |
| Powered | Class A/B/C | Joystick | UE weak, high-dep users |

**Biomechanical advantages of powered wheelchairs:**
- **Reduced upper-extremity load** — eliminates repetitive-strain injuries in shoulder/wrist.
- **Lower cardiovascular demand** — no continuous UE propulsion; energy redirected to cognition/social participation.
- **Greater range & independence** — 25–60 km per charge vs. ~2 km/day manual for chronic paraplegic.
- **Postural stability** — joystick allows 1-hand control; standard in C5–C6 tetraplegics.

**Limitations:**
- High cost (₹ 80,000 – ₹ 4,00,000).
- Maintenance and battery dependence.
- Reduced trunk/UE exercise; risk of deconditioning.
- Heavier (~ 60–120 kg); difficult to transport without adapted vehicle.
- Not all-terrain; slope limitation per ADA 1:12.

> **Award scheme:** 3 marks classification, 2 marks advantages, 2 marks limitations.

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Step 1 — Compute time of walk**

$$ t = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{50 \text{ m}}{0.5 \text{ m/s}} = 100 \text{ s} $$

**Step 2 — Compute total metabolic energy**

$$ W_{met,total} = P_{met} \times t = 250 \text{ W} \times 100 \text{ s} = 25{,}000 \text{ J} = 25 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 3 — Energy cost per kg per metre**

$$ C_{walk} = \frac{W_{met,total}}{m \cdot d} = \frac{25{,}000}{65 \times 50} = \frac{25{,}000}{3{,}250} $$

$$ C_{walk} \approx 7.69 \text{ J/(kg·m)} $$

> *Reasoning:* This is approximately **double the healthy baseline** (4.0 J/kg/m), indicating inefficient ambulation — typical of frail elderly with walkers.

**Step 4 — Base-of-support sufficiency comment**

- A standard pickup walker offers a BoS of approximately **0.55 × 0.45 m** (length × width).
- The user's combined CoG is roughly at **h = 1.0 m** for a standing adult.
- Static stability margin $\tan \phi = d/h \approx 0.10/1.0 = 0.10 \Rightarrow \phi \approx 5.7°$.
- This is **below the 10° standard** typically required for safe community ambulation.
- **Conclusion:** BoS is *borderline sufficient* for indoor use; unsafe for uneven outdoor terrain. Recommend a **rollator (4-wheeled walker with brakes)** for enhanced stability.

> **Award scheme:** 2 marks time, 1 mark energy, 2 marks cost-per-kg, 2 marks BoS comment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Prosthetics & Orthotics Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **components of a trans-femoral (above-knee) prosthesis** with a labelled functional block diagram. Explain the role of the **knee unit** in gait restoration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthosis (KAFO)** for a 70-kg patient with a left-knee flexion contracture of 15°. The orthosis uses a three-point pressure system. If the corrective forces are applied at moment arms of $r_1 = 0.25$ m, $r_2 = 0.18$ m, and $r_3 = 0.05$ m respectively, with $F_1 = 60$ N, compute the **counter-force $F_3$** at the knee joint line and verify that the **skin-interface pressure** remains safe. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Components of a trans-femoral prosthesis (functional block diagram described in words, as Mermaid diagram is given in Section 4.2):**

1. **Socket** — Custom-molded to the residual limb; transfers load via total-surface bearing or hydrostatic loading. Materials: thermoplastic, carbon-fiber, or laminated resin.
2. **Suspension System** — Suction valve, pin-lock, or lanyard; holds the socket to the residuum.
3. **Knee Unit** — Single-axis, polycentric, hydraulic, pneumatic, or microprocessor (C-Leg, Genium). Modulates swing-phase flexion and stance-phase stability.
4. **Pylon (Shank)** — Lightweight tube (aluminium, titanium, carbon-fiber) connecting knee to foot.
5. **Foot Module** — SACH (Solid Ankle Cushioned Heel), single-axis, multi-axial, or dynamic-response carbon-fiber feet.
6. **Cosmetic Cover** — Foam shaping to mimic contralateral limb.

**Role of the knee unit in gait restoration:**

- **Stance phase (heel-strike to toe-off):** The knee must be **stable** under axial load; it resists unwanted flexion to prevent buckling.
- **Swing phase (toe-off to heel-strike):** The knee must **flex** to clear the foot from the ground; the swing-phase flexion is approximately 60–70° at mid-swing.
- **Microprocessor-controlled knees** (e.g., Ottobock C-Leg) use real-time sensors to vary damping with walking speed, terrain, and stair descent.
- **Polycentric knees** (e.g., 4-bar) shift the instantaneous centre of rotation posteriorly during flexion, mimicking natural knee kinematics — improving sit-to-stand transitions.

> **Award scheme:** 4 marks for component list with roles, 3 marks for knee-unit biomechanics.

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Step 1 — Set up the three-point force equation**

$$ F_1 \cdot r_1 - F_2 \cdot r_2 - F_3 \cdot r_3 = 0 $$

Given $F_1 = 60$ N, $r_1 = 0.25$ m, $r_2 = 0.18$ m, $r_3 = 0.05$ m.

**Step 2 — Solve for the counter-force $F_3$**

By the three-point principle, the proximal and distal corrective forces ($F_1$, $F_2$) act in the same direction to produce a flexion-correcting extension moment, while the counter-force $F_3$ at the knee joint line opposes them.

$$ F_3 = \frac{F_1 r_1 + F_2 r_2}{r_3} $$

Assuming $F_1 = F_2 = 60$ N (equal-and-opposite outer couple):

$$ F_3 = \frac{(60)(0.25) + (60)(0.18)}{0.05} $$

$$ F_3 = \frac{15.0 + 10.8}{0.05} = \frac{25.8}{0.05} $$

$$ F_3 = 516 \text{ N} $$

**Step 3 — Compute skin-interface pressure**

Assuming a knee pad contact area $A_{contact} = 25 \text{ cm}^2 = 0.0025 \text{ m}^2$:

$$ P_{skin} = \frac{F_3}{A_{contact}} = \frac{516}{0.0025} = 206{,}400 \text{ Pa} = 206.4 \text{ kPa} $$

**Step 4 — Safety verification**

| Pressure Threshold | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Capillary closure pressure (sustained) | ~ **4.3 kPa** | Landis (1930) |
| Skin tolerance (short-duration) | ~ **100 kPa** | Bader/Cochrane |
| Computed $P_{skin}$ | **206.4 kPa** | This problem |

> **Conclusion:** The computed skin pressure **exceeds the safe threshold of 100 kPa**. The KAFO design is **unsafe** with these parameters. Recommendations:
> - Increase the contact pad area to **at least 50 cm²** to halve the pressure, OR
> - Reduce the outer forces $F_1, F_2$ to **~ 30 N** each, OR
> - Lengthen the moment arm $r_3$ to reduce the required counter-force.

> **Award scheme:** 2 marks for setting up the equation, 2 marks for solving, 1 mark for pressure computation, 2 marks for safety analysis and redesign recommendation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Many students write "$F_3 = 516$ N" and stop. The 2024 valuation key **explicitly awards 2 marks for the safety check and design improvement comment.** Skipping the safety step costs full marks on sub-part (b).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Fire Revision Checklist — KTU OEBMT722 Module 3**

- ✅ A **prosthesis** *replaces*; an **orthosis** *supports*. **Never confuse them.**
- ✅ Wheelchair classes: **A (Indoor)**, **B (Mixed)**, **C (Outdoor)**.
- ✅ ADA ramp slope: **1:12 (4.76°)**; ADA door width: **≥ 815 mm**.
- ✅ Static stability margin: **$\tan \phi = d_{CoG} / h_{CoG}$**.
- ✅ Energy cost formula: **$C = W_{met} / (m \cdot d)$**; units **J/(kg·m)**.
- ✅ Trans-tibial vs trans-femoral: BK has no knee unit, AK does.
- ✅ Myoelectric prostheses use **surface EMG**; body-powered use **cables + harness**.
- ✅ Acronyms to memorize: **FO, AFO, KAFO, HKAFO, KO, HO, WHO, EO, SEWHO**.
- ✅ **Three-Point Pressure Principle:** two outer forces (large moment arm, same direction) + one central counter-force (small moment arm, opposite direction) = net corrective moment.
- ✅ Skin-pressure safety limit: **≤ 100 kPa** for short-duration orthosis wear; **≤ 4.3 kPa** for sustained.
- ✅ Normal cadence: **~ 1.7–2.0 Hz**; normal stride length: **~ 1.4 m**; normal walking speed: **~ 1.3 m/s**.
- ✅ KAFO material hierarchy: **leather+metal (oldest) → thermoplastic → carbon-fiber → 3D-printed** (newest).
- ✅ Microprocessor knee units (C-Leg, Genium) = real-time adaptive damping.
- ✅ **Gait Symmetry Index (SI) = 0% = perfect symmetry**; SI > 10% indicates pathological gait.
- ✅ Rollator walker = 4 wheels + brakes + seat; **safe BoS for community ambulation** in elderly.
- ✅ KTU-favourite viva questions: *"What is the difference between a SACH foot and a dynamic-response foot?"* — SACH = solid, cheap, no articulation; DR = carbon-fiber, energy-storing, high performance.
- ✅ KTU-favourite 2-marker: *"Expand HKAFO"* — answer must read **Hip-Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthosis** verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensory Aids: Hearing Aids, Cochlear Implants, Visual Aids, and Braille Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT722 - ASSISTIVE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensory Aids: Hearing Aids, Cochlear Implants, Visual Aids, and Braille Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: SENSORY AIDS — HEARING, VISION, AND TACTILE RESTORATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sensory Aids (KTU 2024 Definition):** Sensory aids are specialized electronic, electromechanical, and biomedical devices designed to substitute, augment, or replace the function of a damaged or non-functional human sense (hearing, vision, or touch) by converting one form of sensory input into another that the user can perceive.

The four principal sensory aid sub-systems mandated in the **KTU 2024 Scheme OEBMT722 syllabus** are:
1. **Hearing Aids** — electroacoustic amplifiers for mild-to-severe conductive and sensorineural hearing loss.
2. **Cochlear Implants** — neural prostheses that bypass the cochlea and directly stimulate the auditory nerve.
3. **Visual Aids** — optical, electronic, and ultrasonic devices that magnify or convert visual information for low-vision users.
4. **Braille Devices** — refreshable tactile displays that translate digital text into raised-dot patterns readable by touch.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Bionic Translator":** Imagine a foreign tourist who cannot understand the local language. A *translator* converts what the tourist hears (sound) into something they *can* understand (written signs or pictures). A **sensory aid does exactly the same thing for a damaged sense** — it "translates" missing or distorted sensory information (sound waves, light rays, text) into an alternative format (amplified sound, electrical nerve pulses, magnified images, raised dots) that the brain can still interpret.

### Key Standard Metrics (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Sound Pressure Level | **SPL** | **0 dB SPL = 20 µPa** (threshold of hearing) |
| Hearing Aid Gain | **G** | **20 – 60 dB** |
| Hearing Aid Frequency Range | **f** | **200 Hz – 6 kHz** |
| Speech Frequency Band | **f** | **300 Hz – 3.4 kHz** |
| Visual Acuity (Normal) | **V** | **6/6 (or 20/20)** |
| Low Vision Threshold | **V** | **< 6/18 (or < 20/60)** |
| Braille Cell Dot Count | **N** | **6 dots (Grade 1) / 8 dots (Grade 2/Braille 8)** |
| Cochlear Electrode Array | **N** | **12 – 22 electrodes** |
| Cochlear Stimulation Rate | **R** | **500 – 5,000 pulses/sec/electrode** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always remember the reference level — **0 dB SPL = 20 µPa**. Examiners love questions framed around this standard threshold.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HEARING AIDS — System Architecture & Theory

### 2.1.1 Block Diagram of a Hearing Aid

A modern digital hearing aid consists of **five sequential sub-systems**:

1. **Microphone (Transducer)** — converts acoustic pressure waves into a low-voltage analog electrical signal.
2. **Pre-amplifier** — boosts the weak microphone signal (typical gain: **10 – 20 dB**) to a workable voltage.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — samples the analog signal (sampling rate: **16 – 48 kHz**).
4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — applies frequency-shaping, compression, noise reduction, and feedback cancellation.
5. **Receiver (Loudspeaker)** — converts the processed electrical signal back into amplified sound waves coupled to the ear canal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Acoustic Gain Equation:** The acoustic gain of a hearing aid is defined as the difference (in dB) between the **output SPL** delivered to the ear drum and the **input SPL** present at the microphone.

### 2.1.2 Types of Hearing Aids (KTU Mandatory Listing)

| Type | Placement | Power | Indication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Body-Level (Pocket) Aid** | Pocket + earphone | High | Severe loss, elderly |
| **Behind-The-Ear (BTE)** | Behind pinna | Medium–High | Mild–Severe loss |
| **In-The-Ear (ITE)** | Concha bowl | Medium | Mild–Moderate loss |
| **In-The-Canal (ITC)** | Ear canal | Low–Medium | Mild–Moderate loss |
| **Completely-In-Canal (CIC)** | Deep canal | Low | Mild loss |
| **Bone-Conduction Aid** | Mastoid bone | High | Conductive loss / otitis media |

## 2.2 COCHLEAR IMPLANTS — Theory of Operation

A **cochlear implant (CI)** is a *bionic ear* that bypasses the damaged hair cells of the cochlea and electrically stimulates the **auditory nerve (CN VIII)** directly.

### 2.2.1 Internal vs External Components

- **External Sound Processor** — worn behind the ear; contains microphone, DSP, and transmitter coil.
- **Internal Implant** — surgically placed; contains receiver-stimulator and **electrode array** (12–22 electrodes) inserted into the scala tympani.
- **Why Direct Electrical Stimulation?** Because hair cells are destroyed, acoustic amplification is useless. The CI converts sound into **pulsed electrical currents** that the nerve fibers interpret as sound.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "Skip the Damaged Wire":** If the copper wire from a loudspeaker to its cone is broken, simply turning up the amplifier (a hearing aid) won't help. You must **replace the wire** (a cochlear implant) so the electrical signal reaches the speaker (auditory nerve) directly.

### 2.2.2 Signal Processing Strategy (KTU Theory)

| Strategy | Acronym | Full Form | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CIS** | Continuous Interleaved Sampling | Avoids channel interaction | Most modern CIs |
| **SPEAK** | Spectral Peak | Extracts spectral peaks | Cochlear/Nucleus |
| **ACE** | Advanced Combination Encoder | Hybrid of CIS + SPEAK | Cochlear Freedom |
| **FSP / FS4** | Fine Structure Processing | Preserves fine timing info | MED-EL |

## 2.3 VISUAL AIDS — Optical & Electronic Sub-Systems

### 2.3.1 Classification of Visual Aids

1. **Optical Aids** — use lenses and mirrors (magnifiers, telescopes, telemicroscopes).
2. **Non-Optical Aids** — improve contrast, lighting, or use filters (typoscopes, glare filters).
3. **Electronic Aids** — CCTV magnifiers, OCR-based reading machines, screen readers.
4. **Assistive Software** — JAWS, NVDA, ZoomText (note-taking aid for low vision).

### 2.3.2 Magnification Equation (KTU Critical Formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Viewing Distance Principle:** The angular magnification $M$ of a simple magnifier is given by $M = \dfrac{N}{f} + 1$ for a relaxed eye, and $M = \dfrac{D}{f}$ for a *normal* (25 cm) viewing distance, where $D$ is the *near point distance* (**D = 25 cm**).

## 2.4 BRAILLE DEVICES — Tactile Code Translation

### 2.4.1 The Braille Cell

A standard Braille cell consists of **6 raised dots** arranged in a **3 rows × 2 columns** matrix. Each letter of the alphabet is represented by a unique combination of raised dots.

### 2.4.2 Refreshable Braille Displays (KTU Modern Component)

A **refreshable Braille display** uses **piezoelectric actuators** (one per dot) to mechanically raise and lower pins in real time, allowing a deaf-blind user to read digital text on a computer, smartphone, or note-taker.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Variables & Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ dB | $P$ = RMS pressure (Pa), $P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Hearing aid gain problems |
| 2 | Hearing Aid Acoustic Gain | $G = \text{SPL}_{\text{out}} - \text{SPL}_{\text{in}}$ dB | All SPL in dB | Gain calculations |
| 3 | Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ dB | $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ | Acoustic intensity |
| 4 | Simple Magnifier (Normal) | $M = \dfrac{D}{f}$ | $D = 25$ cm, $f$ in cm | Visual aid design |
| 5 | Simple Magnifier (Relaxed Eye) | $M = \dfrac{N}{f} + 1$ | $N$ = viewing distance | Telescope/magnifier |
| 6 | Resolving Power (Eye) | $\theta_{\min} = \dfrac{1.22 \lambda}{D}$ rad | $D$ = pupil diameter | Retinal image formation |
| 7 | Power of a Lens | $P = \dfrac{1}{f}$ dioptre | $f$ in metres | Spectacle lens power |
| 8 | Hearing Loss Classification | Mild: **20–40 dB**, Moderate: **40–70 dB**, Severe: **70–90 dB**, Profound: **> 90 dB** | HL in dB HL | Audiogram interpretation |
| 9 | Nyquist Sampling (ADC) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ | $f_s$ = sample rate | DSP hearing aid |
| 10 | Battery Life (CI) | $t = \dfrac{Q_{\text{bat}}}{I_{\text{stim}}}$ | $Q$ in mAh, $I$ in mA | Cochlear implant design |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the SPL formula, the multiplier is **20** (not 10) because SPL is a *pressure* (field) quantity. Pressure amplitude is proportional to $\sqrt{I}$, so its squared form yields the factor of 20.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Acoustic Gain of a Hearing Aid

**Problem Statement:** A hearing aid has a microphone that produces **5 mV** RMS when the input SPL is **40 dB SPL**. The receiver delivers **110 dB SPL** to the eardrum. Compute the acoustic gain.

**Step 1 — Identify the SPL reference.**

The standard acoustic reference pressure is given by the KTU/ISO standard:

$$
P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the SPL definition to compute the input pressure.**

The SPL formula is a logarithmic ratio, and the **input** pressure $P_{\text{in}}$ corresponding to a 40 dB SPL input is computed by inverting the definition:

$$
\text{SPL}_{\text{in}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_0}\right)
$$

Substitute $\text{SPL}_{\text{in}} = 40$ dB:

$$
40 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

Divide both sides by 20 to simplify:

$$
2 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

Apply the antilogarithm to both sides:

$$
\dfrac{P_{\text{in}}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^{2} = 100
$$

Therefore, the input RMS pressure equals:

$$
P_{\text{in}} = 100 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa} = 2\ \text{mPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the acoustic gain.**

By definition, the acoustic gain $G$ is the difference (in dB) between output SPL and input SPL:

$$
G = \text{SPL}_{\text{out}} - \text{SPL}_{\text{in}}
$$

Substitute the given values $\text{SPL}_{\text{out}} = 110$ dB and $\text{SPL}_{\text{in}} = 40$ dB:

$$
G = 110 - 40 = 70\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The hearing aid amplifies the acoustic signal by **70 dB**, which corresponds to a *ten-thousand-fold* increase in sound pressure ($10^{70/20} = 10^{3.5} \approx 3162$). This is suitable for a **severe hearing loss (70–90 dB HL)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Magnification of a Simple Visual Aid Magnifier

**Problem Statement:** A person with a low-vision acuity of **6/60** wants to read newsprint sized for **6/6 (N6)** vision. Design a simple magnifier of focal length **+5 D** (in dioptres, equivalent to $f = 20$ cm). Find the magnification.

**Step 1 — Convert the lens power to focal length.**

The lens power in dioptres is related to focal length by the standard lensmaker's convention:

$$
P = \dfrac{1}{f\ (\text{metres})}
$$

For a power of $+5$ D, the focal length in metres is:

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{P} = \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.20\ \text{m} = 20\ \text{cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the KTU standard magnifier formula (relaxed eye).**

For a relaxed eye, the angular magnification is given by:

$$
M = \dfrac{N}{f} + 1
$$

where $N$ is the user's *actual near-point distance*. For a low-vision user with **6/60** acuity, $N$ is typically **10 cm** (since the acuity is 10× worse, the near point is 10× closer to maintain a 6/6 retinal image size).

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
M = \dfrac{10\ \text{cm}}{20\ \text{cm}} + 1
$$

This yields:

$$
M = 0.5 + 1 = 1.5
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A magnification of **1.5×** is *insufficient* for 6/60 vision to read 6/6 text. The user would need either a **stronger magnifier** (e.g., $f = 5$ cm giving $M = 3\times$) or an **electronic CCTV magnifier** offering 10×–50×.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Hearing Aid vs. Cochlear Implant (KTU Comparison)

| Parameter | Hearing Aid | Cochlear Implant |
|---|---|---|
| Indication | Mild–Severe SNHL / Conductive | Severe–Profound SNHL |
| Hair Cell Status | Partially intact | Largely destroyed |
| Site of Action | External ear → cochlea | Auditory nerve (CN VIII) |
| Power Source | Zinc-air battery (**1.4 V, 100 mAh**) | Li-ion rechargeable (**3.7 V**) |
| Surgical? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Cost (India, approx.) | ₹5,000 – ₹2,00,000 | ₹5,00,000 – ₹25,00,000 |
| Speech Understanding in Quiet | 80–90 % | 70–80 % |
| Speech Understanding in Noise | 50–60 % | 40–50 % |
| Frequency Resolution | Continuous (analog) | Limited by **N = 12–22 electrodes** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Digital Hearing Aid Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Acoustic Input] --> B[MEMS Microphone]
    B --> C[Pre Amplifier 10 to 20 dB]
    C --> D[Analog to Digital Converter 16 to 48 kHz]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    E --> F[Digital to Analog Converter]
    F --> G[Receiver Speaker]
    G --> H[Output to Ear Canal]
    E -. Feedback Cancellation .-> E
    E -. Noise Reduction .-> E
    E -. Compression .-> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cochlear Implant Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT[External Processor Worn Behind Ear]
        S1[Microphone] --> S2[DSP Sound Processor]
        S2 --> S3[RF Transmitter Coil]
    end
    subgraph INT[Internal Implant Surgical]
        S4[Receiver Stimulator Coil] --> S5[Decoding Circuit]
        S5 --> S6[Current Source Stimulator]
        S6 --> S7[Electrode Array 12 to 22 Channels]
    end
    subgraph COCH[Cochlea Scala Tympani]
        S7 --> N1[Auditory Nerve Fibres CN VIII]
        N1 --> N2[Brainstem Cochlear Nucleus]
    end
    S3 -. RF Inductive Link .-> S4
    S2 -. CIS or SPEAK or ACE Strategy .-> S3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Visual Aid CCTV Magnifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Printed Text Object] --> CAM[CCD or CMOS Camera]
    CAM --> AD[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    AD --> DSP2[Digital Image Processor]
    DSP2 --> ZOOM[Variable Zoom 3x to 50x]
    ZOOM --> CON[Contrast Enhancement]
    CON --> DISP[High Resolution Monitor or Head Mounted Display]
    DISP --> EYE[User Eye]
    DSP2 -. White on Black Mode .-> CON
    DSP2 -. OCR Read Aloud .-> SPK[Speaker]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Refreshable Braille Display

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TXT[Digital Text Source Computer or Phone] --> CONV[Braille Translation Engine Unicode to Braille]
    CONV --> ACT[Driver Circuit for Piezoelectric Pins]
    ACT --> RAISE[Pins Rise to Form Raised Dots]
    ACT --> LOWER[Pins Lower for Next Cell]
    RAISE --> FING[User Finger Reads Cell]
    LOWER --> FING
    FING -. Haptic Feedback .-> ACT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between conductive and sensorineural hearing loss. Which one is treated by a hearing aid and which by a cochlear implant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Conductive Hearing Loss | Sensorineural Hearing Loss |
|---|---|---|
| Site of Lesion | Outer or middle ear | Cochlea (hair cells) or auditory nerve |
| Cause | Wax, otitis media, otosclerosis, perforated eardrum | Noise exposure, ageing (presbycusis), ototoxic drugs, genetic |
| Pathology | Sound *cannot reach* the cochlea properly | Sound *reaches* cochlea but is *not transduced* |
| Treatment | **Hearing aid** (amplifies sound to overcome obstruction) | **Hearing aid (mild) / Cochlear implant (severe)** |
| Reversibility | Often medically/surgically curable | Usually permanent |

(1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for site, 1 mark for treatment mapping.)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the six dots of a standard Braille cell and explain how the letter "D" is encoded.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The standard Braille cell is a **3 rows × 2 columns** matrix of dots numbered:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 4 \\
2 & 5 \\
3 & 6
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The letter **"D"** is encoded by raising dots **1, 4, and 5** (top-left, top-right, middle-right). (1 mark for cell structure, 1 mark for numbering, 1 mark for the encoding.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

### **Question A — Hearing Aid Design (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a digital hearing aid and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the SPL formula, compute the input sound pressure corresponding to a 60 dB SPL input, and the gain of a hearing aid that delivers 120 dB SPL to the eardrum. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks)**

```
[Acoustic Input] → [MEMS Mic] → [Pre-Amp] → [ADC] → [DSP] → [DAC] → [Receiver] → [Ear Canal]
                       ↓             ↓         ↓        ↓
                  Transducer     Boost      Sample   Filter / Compress /
                  Conversion    10-20 dB    16-48kHz  Noise Reduction
```

* **Microphone (1 mark):** Converts acoustic pressure variations into an analog voltage signal. Modern units use **MEMS** technology for small size and low noise.
* **Pre-Amplifier (1 mark):** Boosts the weak microphone output (typically 5–10 mV) to a level suitable for the ADC. Gain: 10–20 dB.
* **ADC (1 mark):** Samples the analog signal at $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$. For speech, $f_s = 16$ kHz is sufficient.
* **DSP (2 marks):** Performs **multi-channel compression**, **wide dynamic range compression (WDRC)**, **digital feedback cancellation (DFC)**, and **directional microphone processing** to improve SNR.
* **DAC + Receiver (1 mark):** Converts the processed digital signal back to analog, then to sound waves via a miniature loudspeaker placed in the ear canal.
* **Power Source (1 mark):** Zinc-air button cell (1.4 V, 100 mAh) lasting 5–14 days.

**(b) SPL Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the SPL formula.

$$
\text{SPL} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right) \quad \text{where}\ P_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Substitute SPL = 60 dB and solve for $P$.

$$
60 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
3 = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

$$
\dfrac{P}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 10^{3} = 1000
$$

$$
P = 1000 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 0.02\ \text{Pa} = 20\ \text{mPa}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the gain formula.

$$
G = \text{SPL}_{\text{out}} - \text{SPL}_{\text{in}}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute.

$$
G = 120 - 60 = 60\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude. A **60 dB** gain classifies this as a *high-power BTE* device suitable for **moderate-to-severe** hearing loss (40–70 dB HL).

---

### **Question B — Cochlear Implant & Visual Aids (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a cochlear implant with a neat block diagram. Why is it preferred over a hearing aid for profound sensorineural loss? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A low-vision patient has a near-point distance of **50 cm** and uses a magnifier of focal length **+10 D**. Compute the magnification for a relaxed eye. Comment on its adequacy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Cochlear Implant — Working Principle (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Introduction. A cochlear implant is a *neural prosthesis* for severe-to-profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss where the **hair cells of the organ of Corti are destroyed** but the auditory nerve is intact.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** External processor.
* Microphone picks up sound → DSP encodes using **CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling)** or **ACE** strategy → encoded signal is sent via an **inductive RF link** (5 MHz) to the internal coil.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Internal implant.
* Receiver-stimulator decodes the RF signal → current sources deliver **biphasic charge-balanced pulses** to the **multi-channel electrode array** (12–22 electrodes) inserted into the **scala tympani** of the cochlea.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Auditory nerve stimulation. Each electrode stimulates a *tonotopically distinct* region (apex = low frequency, base = high frequency), giving the brain a frequency-place map.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Why not a hearing aid? A hearing aid *amplifies* sound, but if hair cells are destroyed, no transduction occurs and the amplified acoustic signal cannot be encoded. The CI *bypasses* the hair cells and directly stimulates the nerve.

**(b) Magnification Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert lens power to focal length.

$$
P = 10\ \text{D} \Rightarrow f = \dfrac{1}{10}\ \text{m} = 10\ \text{cm}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the relaxed-eye magnifier formula.

$$
M = \dfrac{N}{f} + 1
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Substitute $N = 50$ cm and $f = 10$ cm.

$$
M = \dfrac{50}{10} + 1
$$

$$
M = 5 + 1 = 6
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Interpretation. A **6×** magnification is *moderately good* and would allow a low-vision user to read standard newsprint (N6) if the text is held at the magnifier's focal plane.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Adequacy. For 6/60 vision, 6× is *borderline* adequate; for reading finer text (N8, N10), a **CCTV or head-mounted electronic magnifier** providing 10×–25× would be more appropriate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **SPL Formula:** Students often wrongly use $10 \log$ instead of $20 \log$. Remember: **SPL uses 20** because it is a *pressure* (field) quantity, not intensity.
> 2. **Magnifier Formula:** Do *not* confuse $M = N/f$ (relaxed) with $M = D/f$ (normal). For $D = 25$ cm, both are very different.
> 3. **Hearing Aid vs CI:** Examiners deduct marks if students say a hearing aid is used for *profound* loss — that requires a CI.
> 4. **Braille:** Spelling "Brail" instead of "Braille" costs ½ mark in long answers.
> 5. **Units:** Always quote SPL in **dB SPL**, not just "dB". A pure dB is dimensionless; dB SPL is *with reference to* 20 µPa.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Sensory aids** translate one form of sensory signal into another — *amplify, bypass, or substitute* the damaged sense.
- **Hearing aid** = external acoustic amplifier; **Cochlear implant** = direct electrical stimulation of CN VIII.
- **Reference threshold:** **0 dB SPL = 20 µPa**; **0 dB HL = audiometric zero** (frequency-dependent).
- **Hearing loss classification** (in dB HL): Mild 20–40, Moderate 40–70, Severe 70–90, Profound > 90.
- **SPL formula** uses a factor of **20** (pressure), while intensity level uses **10**.
- **Cochlear implant** strategies: **CIS, SPEAK, ACE, FS4** — remember the acronyms.
- **Cochlear electrode array** has **12–22 channels**; placed in **scala tympani**.
- **Visual aids** can be optical (lenses), non-optical (filters), or electronic (CCTV, OCR).
- **Magnification formulas** (relaxed eye: $M = N/f + 1$; normal eye: $M = D/f$ where $D = 25$ cm).
- **Lens power** $P$ in dioptres = $1/f$ (in metres).
- **Braille cell** = 3 × 2 dot matrix; **6 dots standard, 8 dots in computer Braille**.
- **Refreshable Braille displays** use **piezoelectric actuators**.
- **KTU Loves:** Block diagrams — always draw them neat, with arrows, even for 3-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cognitive Aids: Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) Devices, Memory Aids, and Software Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBMT722 - ASSISTIVE DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cognitive Aids: Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) Devices, Memory Aids, and Software Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Cognitive Aids?

**Cognitive Aids** are any device, tool, or strategy that helps a person with cognitive impairments (such as those arising from traumatic brain injury, stroke, dementia, autism, intellectual disability, or age-related decline) to perform mental tasks that they would otherwise find difficult or impossible. These aids work by **offloading cognitive demands** from the impaired brain to an external support system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A cognitive aid is an *assistive technology device* designed to bypass, compensate for, or augment impaired cognitive functions — including attention, memory, executive function, language, and problem-solving — by providing external structure, prompts, reminders, or alternative communication pathways.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Under the KTU 2024 scheme, cognitive aids are broadly classified into three functional families:
> 1. **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) Devices** — for expressive/receptive language support
> 2. **Memory Aids** — for prospective and retrospective memory compensation
> 3. **Cognitive Software Tools** — for skill retraining, task organization, and compensatory strategy delivery

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The External Brain on Your Desk"**
>
> Imagine your brain is a busy office with a small whiteboard (working memory) and a cluttered filing cabinet (long-term memory). When the filing cabinet breaks down, you cannot remember appointments, names, or steps in a recipe. A **cognitive aid** acts like a *smart personal assistant* sitting beside you:
> - A **calendar reminder app** is like the assistant tapping you on the shoulder saying *"Meeting at 3 PM"*.
> - A **picture exchange card** is like the assistant handing you a picture of a coffee cup when you cannot find the word "drink".
> - A **task-sequencing app** is like the assistant reading out checklist steps in the right order.
>
> In short: **Cognitive Aids transform invisible mental tasks into visible, external information that can be perceived and acted upon.**

---

### 1.3 Three Pillars of Cognitive Assistive Technology

> [!NOTE]
> **Pillar 1 — AAC Devices:** Support or replace speech/writing for individuals with communication impairments (e.g., autism, cerebral palsy, aphasia, ALS).
>
> **Pillar 2 — Memory Aids:** Compensate for memory loss by storing information externally and triggering retrieval at the right time (e.g., pill reminders, GPS wayfinders, voice recorders).
>
> **Pillar 3 — Software Tools:** Digital applications designed for cognitive rehabilitation, executive function training, or compensatory learning (e.g., cognitive training apps, mind-mapping software, scheduling platforms).

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics in Cognitive Aids

- **Cognitive Load Reduction (CLR):** The degree to which a device reduces intrinsic cognitive demand on the user, measured subjectively via the NASA-TLX scale.
- **Response Latency:** The time delay between the user initiating a request and the device producing an output. Industry benchmark for high-tech AAC: **< 200 ms** for voice output.
- **User Acceptance Rate (UAR):** Percentage of target users who adopt the device long-term (>6 months). Industry target: **> 70%**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** For electronic memory aids, the reliability standard is typically **> 5,000 hours** of operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) — A Framework

AAC refers to all forms of communication (other than oral speech) used to express thoughts, needs, wants, and ideas. The American Speech-Language-Hearing Association (ASHA) classifies AAC into **unaided** and **aided** systems.

#### 2.1.1 Operational Logic — How an AAC Device Works

1. **Input Stage:** The user activates a switch, touches a screen, points to a symbol, or uses eye-tracking to select a target.
2. **Encoding Stage:** The input is mapped to a semantic symbol, word, or phrase stored in a pre-loaded vocabulary database.
3. **Output Stage:** The system generates the response — either as:
   - **Digitized speech** (recorded human voice), or
   - **Synthesized speech** (text-to-speech engine), or
   - **Visual display** (text on screen for receptive partner).
4. **Feedback Loop:** Auditory/visual confirmation ensures the user that the correct message was sent.

#### 2.1.2 Why and How Each Step Matters

- **Why Input matters:** Users with motor impairments need access methods matched to their residual movement (a blink, a sip, a head-turn).
- **Why Encoding matters:** Symbol sets must match the user's cognitive/linguistic level (concrete photos for non-readers; text for literate users).
- **Why Output matters:** The communication partner must be able to *perceive* the message — choosing the right output modality is as critical as input.

---

### 2.2 Classification of AAC — The KTU High-Yield Table

| Category | Sub-Type | Examples | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unaided AAC** | No equipment | Gestures, manual signs (ASL/ISL), facial expressions | Mild communication delay |
| **Aided — Low-Tech** | Paper-based | PECS (Picture Exchange Communication System), communication boards/charts | Pre-literate children, autism |
| **Aided — High-Tech — Dedicated** | Speech-generating devices (SGDs) | Tobii Dynavox, PRC Accent, ProxTalker | Severe speech impairment (ALS, CP) |
| **Aided — High-Tech — Multi-Purpose** | Tablets/apps | Proloquo2Go, TouchChat, LAMP Words for Life, Avaz | Mobile, flexible AAC needs |
| **Aided — Eye-Gaze** | Eye-tracking AAC | Tobii I-Series, Alea | Locked-in syndrome, advanced ALS |
| **Aided — Brain-Computer Interface (BCI)** | Neural signal AAC | Synchron Stentrode, Neuralink experimental | Total locked-in syndrome |

---

### 2.3 Memory Aids — Theoretical Breakdown

Memory is broadly classified into:
- **Prospective Memory** — remembering to do something in the future ("take medication at 8 PM")
- **Retrospective Memory** — remembering past events, facts, or names

Cognitive memory aids **compensate** for failing internal systems by externalizing these tasks.

#### 2.3.1 Operational Logic of a Memory Aid

1. **Trigger Definition:** The user or caregiver defines *what* to remember and *when*.
2. **Storage:** Information is stored in an external device (paper, smartphone, smart speaker).
3. **Cueing:** At the prescribed time, the system delivers a *multimodal cue* (auditory beep + visual flash + vibration).
4. **Confirmation:** The user acknowledges the cue; if no response, an **escalation protocol** notifies a caregiver.
5. **Logging:** A digital log records completion for clinician review.

#### 2.3.2 Classification of Memory Aids

| Type | Technology Level | Example | Cognitive Demand on User |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External — Non-electronic** | Low-tech | Wall calendar, sticky notes, pillbox | Low |
| **External — Electronic** | Mid-tech | Talking alarm clock, vibrating watch | Medium |
| **External — Smart** | High-tech | Smart speaker reminders (Alexa), phone apps (Medisafe) | Medium-High |
| **Internal — Mnemonic** | Strategy-based | Visual imagery, chunking, method of loci | High (training needed) |
| **Internal — Retrieval Cues** | Strategy-based | Spaced repetition (Anki), context reinstatement | High |
| **Neuroprosthetic (Experimental)** | Implanted | Hippocampal prosthetics (DARPA RAM project) | N/A (clinical trials) |

---

### 2.4 Cognitive Software Tools — Engineering Analysis

#### 2.4.1 What is a Cognitive Software Tool?

A **cognitive software tool** is any digital application (mobile, web, or desktop) that supports or rehabilitates one or more cognitive domains. These tools fall into two broad categories:

- **Compensatory Tools** — Help the user *work around* the impairment (e.g., Google Calendar for memory, Otter.ai for note-taking).
- **Restorative Tools** — Aim to *retrain* the impaired function through repeated practice (e.g., Lumosity, CogniFit, BrainHQ).

#### 2.4.2 Operational Pipeline of a Cognitive Software Tool

1. **User Profiling** — Captures baseline cognitive ability (attention span, working memory capacity, processing speed).
2. **Task Generation** — Algorithms produce calibrated challenges (e.g., n-back tasks at adaptive difficulty).
3. **Engagement Engine** — Gamification, reinforcement, and reward systems maintain motivation.
4. **Performance Analytics** — Logs reaction time, accuracy, error patterns.
5. **Adaptive Feedback** — Difficulty is dynamically adjusted using **Item Response Theory (IRT)** to maintain an 80% success rate (the "flow zone").

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are KTU high-yield for numerical problems in Module 3.**

**Formula 1: Cognitive Load Reduction (CLR)**

$$\text{CLR} = \frac{L_{\text{baseline}} - L_{\text{aided}}}{L_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100\%$$

where $L_{\text{baseline}}$ = cognitive load without aid (NASA-TLX score) and $L_{\text{aided}}$ = cognitive load with aid.

---

**Formula 2: Communication Rate (Words per Minute — WPM)**

$$\text{WPM} = \frac{N_{\text{words}}}{T_{\text{input}} + T_{\text{processing}} + T_{\text{output}}}$$

For typical speech: WPM $\approx$ **150–200**. For high-tech AAC: WPM $\approx$ **10–30**. This efficiency gap is the central engineering challenge of AAC.

---

**Formula 3: AAC Selection Cost-Effectiveness**

$$\text{CE} = \frac{\Delta \text{QALY}}{\text{Cost}_{\text{device}} + \text{Cost}_{\text{training}}}$$

where $\Delta$QALY = Quality-Adjusted Life Year gained, $\text{Cost}_{\text{device}}$ = unit cost of the AAC device, $\text{Cost}_{\text{training}}$ = cost of user/caregiver training.

---

**Formula 4: Memory Aid Reliability (Availability)**

$$A_{\text{system}} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

where MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair. Industry benchmark for memory aids: $A_{\text{system}} \geq 0.999$ (99.9%).

---

**Formula 5: BCI Information Transfer Rate (ITR)**

$$\text{ITR} = \frac{\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2\!\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right)}{T}$$

where $N$ = number of classes, $P$ = classification accuracy, $T$ = trial duration in minutes. Units: **bits/min**.

---

**Formula 6: Adaptive Difficulty Adjustment (Item Response Theory)**

$$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \alpha \cdot (R - 0.80)$$

where $\theta$ = user ability estimate, $\alpha$ = learning rate constant, $R$ = success rate. Target $R = 0.80$ to maintain flow state.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Cognitive Aid Type | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|
| Pediatric autism intervention | PECS, LAMP AAC | Accelerates functional speech acquisition by **40%** |
| Post-stroke aphasia rehabilitation | Lingraphica TouchTalk | Restores communication in **60%** of chronic aphasia patients |
| Alzheimer's home care | Alexa + Medisafe reminder chain | Reduces caregiver burden by **30%** |
| Traumatic brain injury (TBI) rehab | NeuroPage reminder system | Improves task completion from **40% → 85%** |
| Cerebral palsy | Eye-gaze AAC (Tobii) | Enables **15–25 WPM** in non-speaking users |
