| Locked-in syndrome | BCI-AAC | Restores basic yes/no communication at **> 90% accuracy** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cognitive Load Reduction (CLR) — Worked Example

**Problem Statement:** A stroke survivor scores **85/100** on the NASA-TLX scale while performing a meal-preparation task without any aid. After training with a task-sequencing app, the score drops to **40/100**. Calculate the percentage reduction in cognitive load.

**Step 1: Identify given values**

$$L_{\text{baseline}} = 85, \quad L_{\text{aided}} = 40$$

**Step 2: Apply the CLR formula**

$$\text{CLR} = \frac{L_{\text{baseline}} - L_{\text{aided}}}{L_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$\text{CLR} = \frac{85 - 40}{85} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator**

$$\text{CLR} = \frac{45}{85} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5: Divide**

$$\text{CLR} = 0.5294 \times 100\%$$

**Step 6: Final result**

$$\boxed{\text{CLR} \approx 52.94\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The task-sequencing app reduced the user's cognitive load by approximately **53%**, indicating high clinical utility and strong user benefit. KTU examiners typically award full marks if the unit (%) is explicitly stated.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Communication Rate Comparison — AAC vs. Natural Speech

**Problem Statement:** A high-tech AAC user takes **2.5 seconds** to select a 3-word message using a 2-switch scanning interface, with a **0.3 s** processing delay and a **0.5 s** output latency. Calculate the WPM and compare with natural speech at **180 WPM**.

**Step 1: Compute total time per message**

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{input}} + T_{\text{processing}} + T_{\text{output}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$T_{\text{total}} = 2.5 + 0.3 + 0.5 = 3.3 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Apply WPM formula**

$$\text{WPM} = \frac{N_{\text{words}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 60 \text{ s/min}$$

**Step 4: Substitute**

$$\text{WPM} = \frac{3}{3.3} \times 60$$

**Step 5: Compute**

$$\text{WPM} = 0.909 \times 60 \approx 54.5 \text{ WPM}$$

**Step 6: Compute efficiency ratio vs. natural speech**

$$\text{Efficiency Ratio} = \frac{54.5}{180} \times 100\% \approx 30.3\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The efficiency gap of ~70% is the **driving design challenge** in modern AAC — motivating research into word prediction, AI-based language modeling (e.g., GPT-augmented AAC), and dynamic layout optimization.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Matching Cognitive Aid to User Profile

The selection of a cognitive aid is **never one-size-fits-all**. The following decision matrix maps user characteristics to recommended aid types.

| User Profile | Primary Impairment | Recommended Cognitive Aid | Rationale (Step-by-Step) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Child (3–6 yrs) with autism, non-verbal | Communication | PECS (low-tech) → LAMP (high-tech) | Step 1: Start with picture exchange. Step 2: Assess symbol comprehension. Step 3: Upgrade to speech-generating app once motor control is established. |
| Adult post-stroke with aphasia | Expressive language | Lingraphica TouchTalk / Proloquo2Go | Step 1: Assess residual literacy. Step 2: Choose text-based or icon-based interface. Step 3: Train with SLP for 8–12 weeks. |
| Elderly with mild dementia | Prospective memory | Alexa / Google Home reminders | Step 1: Identify critical routines (medication, meals). Step 2: Set voice cues with redundancy (visual flashing light). Step 3: Add caregiver escalation for missed doses. |
| Person with severe cerebral palsy, quadriplegia | Motor + speech | Eye-gaze AAC (Tobii I-13) | Step 1: Calibrate eye tracker. Step 2: Build personalized vocabulary grid. Step 3: Conduct partner training. |
| Locked-in patient (ALS) | Total motor paralysis | BCI-AAC (Synchron Stentrode, investigational) | Step 1: Surgical implantation of electrode. Step 2: Machine learning calibration of neural decoder. Step 3: Continuous monitoring. |
| TBI survivor with executive dysfunction | Planning, sequencing | Google Calendar + task app (e.g., Tiimo) | Step 1: Break tasks into sub-steps. Step 2: Use visual schedules. Step 3: Daily review with occupational therapist. |
| Dyslexic student | Reading/writing | Text-to-speech (NaturalReader), speech-to-text (Dragon) | Step 1: Identify barrier modality. Step 2: Install dual-modality software. Step 3: Verify in classroom setting. |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Adaptive Difficulty Engine in Cognitive Software

**Problem Statement:** A cognitive training app uses an IRT-based difficulty engine. After the 5th trial, the user's ability estimate is $\theta_5 = 0.6$ and success rate is $R = 0.70$. The learning rate constant is $\alpha = 0.5$. Compute $\theta_6$.

**Step 1: Apply the adaptive update formula**

$$\theta_{n+1} = \theta_n + \alpha \cdot (R - 0.80)$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$\theta_6 = 0.6 + 0.5 \cdot (0.70 - 0.80)$$

**Step 3: Compute the difference**

$$\theta_6 = 0.6 + 0.5 \cdot (-0.10)$$

**Step 4: Multiply**

$$\theta_6 = 0.6 + (-0.05)$$

**Step 5: Final result**

$$\boxed{\theta_6 = 0.55}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Because the user's success rate (70%) is below the target (80%), the engine *decreases* task difficulty for the next trial. This keeps the user in the optimal learning zone and prevents frustration-induced dropout — a critical engineering feature for cognitive rehabilitation software.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: BCI Information Transfer Rate

**Problem Statement:** A P300-based BCI speller has **36 classes** (26 letters + 10 digits), achieves a classification accuracy of **0.85**, with each trial taking **2 seconds**. Compute the ITR in bits/min.

**Step 1: Write the ITR formula**

$$\text{ITR} = \frac{\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2\!\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right)}{T}$$

**Step 2: Substitute $N = 36$, $P = 0.85$, $T = 2$ s = $2/60$ min**

**Step 3: Compute $\log_2 36$**

$$\log_2 36 \approx 5.17 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4: Compute $P \log_2 P$**

$$0.85 \cdot \log_2 0.85 \approx 0.85 \cdot (-0.2345) \approx -0.1993$$

**Step 5: Compute $(1-P)\log_2\!\left(\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right)$**

$$0.15 \cdot \log_2\!\left(\frac{0.15}{35}\right) = 0.15 \cdot \log_2(0.00429) \approx 0.15 \cdot (-7.866) \approx -1.180$$

**Step 6: Sum numerator**

$$5.17 - 0.1993 - 1.180 \approx 3.79 \text{ bits/trial}$$

**Step 7: Convert to bits/min (30 trials/min)**

$$\text{ITR} = 3.79 \times 30 \approx 113.7 \text{ bits/min}$$

**Step 8: Final result**

$$\boxed{\text{ITR} \approx 113.7 \text{ bits/min}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** Typical P300 spellers achieve **20–60 bits/min** in practice due to noise, fatigue, and BCI-illiteracy. The calculated value represents an *optimistic* upper bound. Modern research targets **> 100 bits/min** to make BCI-AAC practically viable.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
