## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Mindset:** This section provides the *quantitative* foundation expected at KTU board level. Three canonical derivations are worked out exhaustively: (i) battery life, (ii) Shannon capacity for wearable ECG telemetry, and (iii) Kalman filter state prediction for IMU-based fall detection.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Battery Life of a Wearable Fall Detector

**Problem Statement (typical KTU-style):**
A wearable fall-detection wristband operates from a 3.7 V, 400 mAh Li-Po battery. The system has three operating modes:
- *Active sensing:* 50 mA current, $V_{DD}$ = 3.3 V.
- *Sleep (low-power):* 0.5 mA.
- *Radio transmit (BLE burst):* 20 mA, duration 10 ms, every 1 s.

Calculate the average current draw and the operational battery life in hours.

**Step 1 — Identify duty cycles of each mode.**
- Active sensing: always ON, duty cycle = 1.0
- Sleep mode: not used in this design (system always active for safety)
- Radio transmit: ON for 10 ms every 1000 ms → duty cycle = $10/1000 = 0.01$

**Step 2 — Compute time-averaged current $I_{avg}$.**
Use the linear averaging principle:

$$I_{avg} = \sum_i I_i \cdot d_i$$

where $d_i$ is the duty cycle of mode $i$.

$$I_{avg} = (50 \text{ mA} \times 1.0) + (20 \text{ mA} \times 0.01) + (0.5 \text{ mA} \times 0)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$I_{avg} = 50 + 0.2 + 0 = 50.2 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Apply battery life formula.**

$$T = \frac{C}{I_{avg}} = \frac{400 \text{ mAh}}{50.2 \text{ mA}}$$

$$T = 7.968 \text{ hours} \approx 8 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5 — Power verification.**

$$P_{avg} = V_{DD} \times I_{avg} = 3.3 \text{ V} \times 50.2 \text{ mA} = 165.66 \text{ mW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** 8 hours is *insufficient* for a medical-grade wearable. To achieve the KTU-design target of $\ge$ 72 hours, the designer must either (a) increase battery to $\ge$ 3600 mAh, (b) reduce active current via duty-cycled sensing (e.g., 10% duty), or (c) integrate energy harvesting.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shannon Capacity for Wearable ECG Telemetry

**Problem Statement:**
An ECG wearable streams 3-lead ECG at a sampling rate of 500 samples/s, 12-bit resolution, over a BLE channel of bandwidth 1 MHz, with SNR of 30 dB. Verify that the channel can support the data rate.

**Step 1 — Compute required data rate $R$.**

$$R = f_s \times N_{bits} \times N_{channels} = 500 \times 12 \times 3$$

$$R = 18{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 18 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.**

$$SNR_{linear} = 10^{SNR_{dB}/10} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon-Hartley theorem.**

$$C = B \log_2(1 + SNR_{linear})$$

$$C = 1{,}000{,}000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \text{ bps}$$

$$\log_2(1001) = \log_{10}(1001)/\log_{10}(2) = 3.0004/0.3010 = 9.967 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

$$C = 1 \text{ MHz} \times 9.967 = 9.967 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4 — Compare required vs. available capacity.**

$$R = 18 \text{ kbps} \ll C = 9.967 \text{ Mbps}$$

The channel supports the data with a massive margin (utilization $\approx 0.18\%$), confirming BLE is more than sufficient for ECG telemetry. The bottleneck is therefore **power**, not bandwidth.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Two-State Kalman Filter for IMU Tilt Estimation

**Problem Statement:**
A fall-detection wearable uses a 2D accelerometer to estimate the body's tilt angle $\theta$. The accelerometer measurement model is:

$$z_k = \theta_k + v_k, \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$$

The state evolves as $\theta_k = \theta_{k-1} + w_{k-1}$, with $w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$. Find the steady-state Kalman gain $K_{ss}$ and the predicted tilt.

**Step 1 — State prediction.**

$$\hat{\theta}_{k \vert k-1} = \hat{\theta}_{k-1 \vert k-1}$$

(no change because $A = 1$ in this model).

**Step 2 — Error covariance prediction.**

$$P_{k \vert k-1} = P_{k-1 \vert k-1} + Q$$

**Step 3 — Kalman gain computation.**

$$K_k = \frac{P_{k \vert k-1} H^T}{H P_{k \vert k-1} H^T + R} = \frac{P_{k \vert k-1}}{P_{k \vert k-1} + R}$$

**Step 4 — Update.**

$$\hat{\theta}_{k \vert k} = \hat{\theta}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - \hat{\theta}_{k \vert k-1})$$

$$P_{k \vert k} = (1 - K_k) P_{k \vert k-1}$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state condition.**
At steady state, $P_{k \vert k} = P_{k-1 \vert k-1} = P_{ss}$. Substituting:

$$P_{ss} = (1 - K_{ss})(P_{ss} + Q)$$

$$P_{ss} = P_{ss} + Q - K_{ss}(P_{ss} + Q)$$

$$0 = Q - K_{ss}(P_{ss} + Q)$$

$$K_{ss} = \frac{Q}{P_{ss} + Q}$$

From the gain equation:

$$K_{ss} = \frac{P_{ss}}{P_{ss} + R}$$

Equating:

$$\frac{Q}{P_{ss}+Q} = \frac{P_{ss}}{P_{ss}+R}$$

$$Q(P_{ss} + R) = P_{ss}(P_{ss} + Q)$$

$$QP_{ss} + QR = P_{ss}^2 + QP_{ss}$$

$$QR = P_{ss}^2$$

$$P_{ss} = \sqrt{QR}$$

**Final Result:**

$$K_{ss} = \frac{\sqrt{QR}}{R + \sqrt{QR}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{R/Q}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If sensor noise $R$ is large (noisy IMU), $K_{ss} \to 0$ → filter trusts the model more.
- If process noise $Q$ is large (rapid body motion), $K_{ss} \to 1$ → filter trusts the measurement more.
- This is the mathematical embodiment of the *trade-off* in every wearable sensor-fusion design.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component-vs-Performance Trade-off

| Component | Power Impact | Latency Impact | Reliability | Cost |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|-------------|------|
| BLE radio | Medium | Low (~10 ms) | High | Low |
| Wi-Fi radio | High | Very low (~3 ms) | High | Medium |
| LoRa radio | Very low | High (~1 s) | Medium | Medium |
| Edge MCU (TinyML) | Medium | Very low | High | Low |
| Cloud inference | N/A (transmit only) | High (50–500 ms) | Medium (network dep.) | Pay-per-use |
| Energy harvester (PV) | Positive (extends life) | N/A | Medium | Medium |
| Redundant sensors (IMU + barometer) | Higher | Low | Very high | Higher |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
*Define the Internet of Things (IoT) as applied to assistive devices. List any four wearable sensors commonly used in IoT-based assistive systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The Internet of Things (IoT) in assistive devices is the networked interconnection of physical sensors, actuators, wearable electronics, and smart environmental systems that continuously acquire, process, and exchange physiological and contextual data over the internet, enabling remote monitoring, predictive intervention, and automation of daily living for individuals with disabilities.

> **Four wearable sensors (1 mark — ¼ each):**
> 1. Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU) — accelerometer + gyroscope for gait and fall analysis.
> 2. Photoplethysmography (PPG) sensor — for heart rate and SpO₂.
> 3. Ag/AgCl surface electrodes — for ECG / EMG / EEG bio-signal acquisition.
> 4. Galvanic Skin Response (GSR) sensor — for stress and emotional-state monitoring.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*What is smart home integration in the context of assistive technology? Explain its significance with two examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Smart home integration for assistive technology is the orchestrated deployment of IoT-enabled environmental sensors and actuators that adapt the home environment dynamically to the needs of a person with disability, supporting Activities of Daily Living (ADL) without constant human assistance.

> **Significance (1 mark):** It promotes **independent living**, reduces **caregiver burden**, and provides **24×7 safety monitoring** for elderly and disabled users.

> **Two examples (1 mark — ½ each):**
> 1. **Smart lighting + motion sensors** that automatically illuminate the path from the bed to the bathroom for a visually impaired user at night.
> 2. **Gas-leak + automated shut-off valves** that detect a stove left on by a cognitively impaired user and send an alert to a caregiver while closing the gas supply.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**Question A (14 marks):**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **four-layer IoT architecture** as applied to assistive devices. Explain the function of each layer with suitable examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** A wearable ECG monitor operates from a 3.7 V, 600 mAh battery. Active current = 40 mA, sleep current = 0.8 mA, and BLE transmit current = 25 mA (transmit for 5 ms every 1 s, otherwise sleeping). Calculate the average current, average power dissipation, and battery life in hours.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Four-Layer IoT Architecture [7 marks]**

> **Block diagram (3 marks):** Refer to the layered architecture shown in *Section 4.1* of these notes — draw the four labelled layers with two example components per layer.

> **Layer-wise explanation (4 marks — 1 mark per layer):**

1. **Perception / Sensor Layer:** Acquires raw physical/physiological signals.
   - *Examples:* IMU for fall detection, ECG electrodes for cardiac monitoring, PIR sensor for room occupancy.

2. **Network / Communication Layer:** Reliably transports data to edge/cloud.
   - *Examples:* BLE for body-area network, Wi-Fi for smart-home backhaul, MQTT for cloud telemetry, LoRa for outdoor wayfinding.

3. **Processing / Edge-Cloud Computing Layer:** Performs data fusion, feature extraction, and ML inference.
   - *Examples:* TinyML on STM32 for on-wrist fall classification, smartphone hub for local aggregation, AWS IoT for long-term analytics.

4. **Application / Actuation Layer:** Delivers user-facing services and physical actions.
   - *Examples:* Caregiver mobile dashboard, Alexa voice assistant, smart-lock actuation, haptic feedback on a smart cane.

**(b) Battery Life Calculation [7 marks]**

> **Step 1 — Identify duty cycles (1 mark):**
> - Active: ON for entire 1 s → duty $d_1 = 1.0$
> - Sleep: ON for the remaining 995 ms every 1 s → duty $d_2 = 0.995$
> - Transmit: ON for 5 ms every 1 s → duty $d_3 = 0.005$

> **Step 2 — Average current (2 marks):**

$$I_{avg} = (40 \times 1.0) + (0.8 \times 0.995) + (25 \times 0.005)$$

$$I_{avg} = 40 + 0.796 + 0.125 = 40.921 \text{ mA}$$

> **Step 3 — Average power dissipation (2 marks):**

$$P_{avg} = V_{DD} \times I_{avg} = 3.7 \text{ V} \times 40.921 \text{ mA}$$

$$P_{avg} = 151.41 \text{ mW}$$

> **Step 4 — Battery life (2 marks):**

$$T = \frac{C}{I_{avg}} = \frac{600 \text{ mAh}}{40.921 \text{ mA}} = 14.66 \text{ hours}$$

**Conclusion:** Battery lasts ~14.7 hours, which is acceptable for a single-day use with nightly charging, but falls short of the 72-hour medical-grade target — energy harvesting or larger battery is recommended.

---

**Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Sense–Decide–Actuate closed loop** of a smart home designed for an elderly user. Sketch the block diagram and describe one sensor, one decision rule, and one actuator for each of the following scenarios: (i) fall detection, (ii) medication reminder, (iii) gas-leak safety.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss **energy harvesting techniques** suitable for wearable assistive devices. Compute the energy stored in a 3.7 V, 1200 mAh battery in Wh, and estimate how many hours a 200 mW wearable device can run from it.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Sense–Decide–Actuate Loop [7 marks]**

> **Loop description (2 marks):** The Sense–Decide–Actuate loop is a feedback cycle in which environmental and physiological sensors (Sense) feed data to an edge processor where rule-based and ML-based engines (Decide) infer the appropriate response, which is then executed by physical actuators (Actuate). The actuator state is re-sensed, closing the loop.

> **Block diagram (1 mark):** Refer to *Section 4.3*.

> **Three scenarios (4 marks — ~1.33 marks each):**

| Scenario | Sensor (Sense) | Decision Rule (Decide) | Actuator (Actuate) |
|----------|----------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Fall detection | IMU on wrist (accel. > 2.5 g + sudden orientation change) | "If fall signature AND no recovery in 30 s → emergency" | Smartphone SMS + caregiver call + smart door auto-unlock |
| Medication reminder | Smart pillbox contact sensor | "If 09:00 AM reached AND box unopened → reminder" | Voice prompt via smart speaker + flashing light + phone push notification |
| Gas-leak safety | MQ-135 gas sensor in kitchen | "If LPG concentration > threshold for 5 s → emergency" | Auto gas-valve shut-off + siren + SMS to family + exhaust fan ON |

**(b) Energy Harvesting and Battery Calculation [7 marks]**

> **Energy harvesting discussion (3 marks):**
> 1. *Photovoltaic (PV):* 10–100 mW/cm² in daylight; suited for outdoor smart canes, outdoor fall pendants.
> 2. *Thermoelectric (TEG):* 0.1–1 mW/cm² from body-ambient gradient; suited for continuous-wear wristbands.
> 3. *Piezoelectric:* 1–10 mW per gait cycle from knee or heel strike; suited for active users, prosthetic sockets.
> 4. *RF harvesting:* < 0.1 mW from ambient Wi-Fi; used for ultra-low-power sensors and trickle charging.

> **Energy stored in battery (2 marks):**

$$E = V \times C = 3.7 \text{ V} \times 1200 \text{ mAh}$$

$$E = 3.7 \times 1.2 \text{ Wh} = 4.44 \text{ Wh}$$

> **Operational hours (2 marks):**

$$T = \frac{E}{P} = \frac{4.44 \text{ Wh}}{0.2 \text{ W}}$$

$$T = 22.2 \text{ hours}$$

> **Conclusion:** A 4.44 Wh battery supports a 200 mW wearable for ~22 hours, sufficient for full-day use; combined with a 1 mW TEG harvester, life extends to ~24+ hours with energy-neutral operation.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mAh to Wh** — examiners specifically check unit consistency. A battery life calculation without unit conversion loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing "IoT" with "Internet"** — IoT is a *system of interconnected physical devices*, not just network access. A definition that omits the *physical-device + sensor/actuator* aspect is incomplete.
> 3. **Drawing diagrams without labels** — Mermaid/block diagrams must show sensor → MCU → radio → cloud flow with **all four layers labelled**, not just a single box.
> 4. **Skipping duty-cycle computation** — in any average-power question, students often simply add currents without weighting by duty cycle. This is the **most common 2-mark loss**.
> 5. **Not mentioning security/privacy** — KTU 2024 explicitly values **data privacy, HIPAA/GDPR awareness** in IoT-AT designs. A complete answer must mention at least one security measure (encryption, anonymization, secure boot).
> 6. **No mention of standards** — IEC 60601-1 (medical electrical safety), ISO 13485 (medical device QMS), and IEEE 11073 (personal health device communication) are high-yield keywords.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — IoT in Assistive Devices**

- **Definition triad:** *Sense → Decide → Actuate* is the heart of every IoT-AT system.
- **Four layers:** Perception → Network → Processing → Application. Memorize the layer names and one example component per layer.
- **Key protocols:** **BLE** (body-area), **Wi-Fi** (smart home), **MQTT** (cloud telemetry), **LoRa** (long-range outdoor), **Zigbee/Z-Wave** (home mesh).
- **Power budget:** Wearables < 100 mW; smart home hubs < 5 W; battery life target ≥ 72 h.
- **Battery life formula:** $T = C / I_{avg}$ — always compute $I_{avg}$ as a duty-cycle-weighted sum.
- **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$ — bandwidth, not power, is rarely the bottleneck.
- **Kalman filter:** Steady-state gain $K_{ss} = 1 / (1 + \sqrt{R/Q})$ — high $Q$ trusts sensor; high $R$ trusts model.
- **Smart home rules:** Three primary safety scenarios — *fall, medication, gas-leak* — each with explicit Sense/Decide/Actuate triplet.
- **Standards to mention in answers:** IEC 60601-1, ISO 13485, IEEE 11073, GDPR/HIPAA.
- **Edge vs. Cloud split:** Latency-critical inference (fall, seizure) → on-edge TinyML; long-term trend analytics → cloud.
- **Energy harvesting:** PV (high outdoor), TEG (low continuous), Piezo (gait-driven), RF (trickle).
- **Design workflow:** *Need → Sensors → MCU → PCB → Firmware → ML → App → Cloud → Pilot → Regulatory → Deploy*.
- **Common exam keywords:** "Sense–Decide–Actuate," "Body Area Network (BAN)," "MQTT publish-subscribe," "TinyML edge inference," "OTA firmware update," "OTA" = Over-The-Air.
